title: LinkedIn's Algorithm Killed Company Pages. Employees Are The Distribution Now.
tldr: Argues LinkedIn's algorithm now suppresses brand/company page reach, forcing organizations to rely on employees as the primary distribution channel. A direct prompt to rethink employee advocacy and EGC strategy.
angle: How comms teams should re-architect their owned-channel strategy around employees now that company pages are throttled.

title: Your Best-Ranked Page Might Be Invisible to Google's AI
tldr: Pages ranking high in traditional search may not be cited in Google's AI Overviews, breaking the old assumption that top-10 rankings equal visibility. Critical for content teams rethinking SEO for the AI era.
angle: Why content strategists need a GEO (generative engine optimization) layer on top of SEO, and how to audit for AI citation gaps.

title: The #1 Role Your Content Team Needs in 2026 Is a Managing Editor
tldr: As AI makes content cheap to produce, editorial judgment becomes the differentiator, making the managing editor the key hire. A strong argument for human governance over content volume.
angle: The case for editorial judgment as the scarce skill in AI-saturated content operations.

title: Building a tier-1 media map for new media
tldr: Argues 'tier-1' media no longer means legacy outlets amid 17,000+ media job cuts in 2025, and lays out how to rebuild a media map for a fragmented earned-media landscape. Practical for modern media relations.
angle: How to redraw your media map when newsletters, creators and niche outlets out-influence legacy tier-1s.

title: Why "AI Productivity Gains" Is the Wrong Pitch for Every Stakeholder Above You
tldr: Pitching AI as productivity gains fails to move CMOs, CFOs and legal; the piece shows how to reframe the metric per audience to defend budget and headcount. Useful internal-comms and persuasion framing.
angle: How to translate AI value into each executive's language to win budget for content and comms.

title: The Rundown: AI clones split the creator economy
tldr: Unauthorized AI voice clones and authorized digital twins are dividing creators, brands and lawyers over rights and authenticity. A live issue for anyone working with talent and synthetic media.
angle: The governance and consent playbook brands need before deploying AI clones of creators or spokespeople.

title: What kind of stories turn local news readers into subscribers? Hard news, not soft stuff
tldr: New data shows hard news (government, public health, politics) converts readers to paying subscribers far better than soft content. A data-backed counter to engagement-bait assumptions.
angle: What subscription data on hard vs soft news teaches content strategists about value over virality.

title: The agency holdcos have an AI story, but not an AI business model
tldr: Agency holding companies are pitching AI narratives without a real monetization model behind them, exposing a gap between rhetoric and revenue. Sharp on the AI-hype-vs-reality theme.
angle: Why a compelling AI story isn't a strategy, and what a real AI business model looks like for agencies.

title: Podcasts and influencers least culturally relevant media
tldr: Thinkbox study finds TV holds the largest share of media's cultural influence while podcasts and creators rank lowest, challenging creator-economy hype. Provocative for media-mix debates.
angle: Is the creator economy overrated for cultural impact? Reconciling reach with actual influence.

title: A 6-step framework for boosting social GEO
tldr: A practical framework for creating social content that AI platforms want to cite, as consumers increasingly use AI to research and shop. Actionable for the generative-search visibility shift.
angle: How social content becomes AI-citable: a GEO framework for comms and PR teams.

title: AI disclosure labels don't hurt ad performance: Here's what the numbers say
tldr: Data shows AI-disclosure labels on ads don't degrade performance even as disclosure laws spread, easing brand fears about transparency. Relevant to AI governance and trust.
angle: Why transparency about AI use doesn't cost performance, and what that means for content trust.

title: Five Trends Redefining Brand Storytelling
tldr: Edelman lays out five shifts reshaping how brands tell stories, useful as a synthesized view from a major comms firm. Good fodder for storytelling strategy thinking.
angle: Stress-testing Edelman's storytelling trends against what's actually working in B2B narratives.

title: Hooked on the Scroll: How Vertical Dramas are Changing Brand Storytelling
tldr: Vertical short-form dramas are emerging as a new format brands are using for storytelling, blending entertainment and marketing. A genuinely new format trend worth watching.
angle: Can vertical micro-dramas become a serious brand-storytelling format, or are they a passing gimmick?

title: The Six Stages of PESO Model Maturity
tldr: Maps a six-stage maturity ladder for integrated paid-earned-shared-owned media, finding 91% of teams sit in the bottom half. A useful diagnostic framework for integrated comms operations.
angle: Where most comms teams stall on the PESO maturity ladder and the smallest next move to advance.

title: Canva Paid 5,000 People to Spend a Week Learning AI. They Couldn't Figure Out How to Start.
tldr: Even with paid time and resources, thousands of Canva employees struggled to begin using AI, revealing the real adoption barrier is knowing where to start. A sharp internal-comms and change-management lesson.
angle: Why AI adoption fails on the 'how to start' problem, and what change comms can do about it.
